Taking care of your daughter well doesn’t automatically make someone a feminist.

We’ve seen fathers who love and protect their daughters but treat their wives poorly. A wife is also someone’s daughter. If respect stops with your own child, it’s not a principle — it’s just personal attachment.

Feminism isn’t about loving one woman in your life. It’s about believing women in general deserve equal respect and agency.

So father–daughter love may influence someone, but it has nothing to do with feminism by default. The real question is: does that respect extend to all women, or only to your own daughter? 🤔

comments.text:
Maybe this is your perspective,let me share mine, as a mother living it every day.

Motherhood, for me, has come with very real, quiet sacrifices like sleepless nights, mental load, and putting parts of myself on pause in ways I never imagined.
Not because I see it as a pedestal, but because love sometimes asks for it in ways you only truly understand when you’re in it, day after day.

Even with a nanny, I still don’t really have time for myself and that’s a choice I’ve made, because I want to be present for my son.

Sometimes it also means choosing to stay back because your child isn’t comfortable in new spaces, or because their routine, their feeding, their sense of security comes first… and I’m one of those moms.

At the same time, I feel we need to be a little careful about what we glorify or call a “setting a standard.”
Motherhood is already a journey where we’re constantly judged, told what’s right and what’s not, and made to feel like we’re falling short of some ideal.

For many of us, it doesn’t fit into a ‘no sacrifice’ narrative it’s messy, demanding, and full of quiet trade offs that aren’t always visible. Not idealised, just real.

I don’t think motherhood can be defined so easily without living it fully.
And I don’t think one ‘standard’ or one ‘generation’ of mothers is better than another we’re all just doing our best in very different realities.

At the end of the day, our motherhood isn’t measured by how it looks from the outside, but by the bond we build with our child the safety, the love, the connection only we understand.

And that version of motherhood deserves just as much respect and space 🤍
